overview: Rakdos Tree is a sacrifice-based midrange archetype that leverages efficient creatures and powerful outlets to generate card advantage and direct damage. By cycling through its deck with Agatha's Soul Cauldron and Tree of Perdition, the deck aims to outvalue opponents while maintaining board presence. Thoughtseize provides crucial early disruption to protect the game plan and buy time for the engine to stabilize.
win_conditions: Direct damage from Tree of Perdition sacrifices
Outvaluing opponents with Agatha's Soul Cauldron card draw

keep_criteria: Hands with early interaction like Thoughtseize
Hands containing Tree of Perdition or Agatha's Soul Cauldron with lands
Hands with efficient one- or two-drop creatures
Keep hands with 2-3 lands, Thoughtseize, and at least one of Tree of Perdition or Agatha's Soul Cauldron.
Prioritize hands that can play a land and cast Thoughtseize on turn 1.
mulligan_criteria: Hands lacking lands or early plays
Hands with only high-cost cards and no interaction
Hands missing sacrifice outlets or card draw
Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, no disruption, or only high-cost threats without mana development.

early_game: Play lands and efficient creatures to establish board presence
Use Thoughtseize to disrupt opponent's game plan
Prioritize keeping hands that can deploy Tree or Agatha by turn three
Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize to strip key disruption or early threats.
Turn 2: Develop your mana base, deploy Agatha's Soul Cauldron or a sacrifice outlet, and begin sacrificing low-cost creatures or artifacts to fuel the engine.
Sequence lands to ensure you can hit 2-drop and 3-drop mana bases without flooding, prioritizing dual lands or shock lands if available.
mid_game: Activate Tree of Perdition's sacrifice ability to deal direct damage and thin your deck
Use Agatha's Soul Cauldron to convert excess creatures into card advantage
Maintain board control while cycling through your deck efficiently
Balance mana between developing your engine, casting disruption, and deploying threats.
Prioritize keeping Thoughtseize for critical turns and use the graveyard as a resource rather than a liability.
Sacrifice strategically to maximize Cauldron value without overextending into sweepers.
Commit Tree of Perdition only when you can protect it or have removal ready for key threats.
Hold back on overcommitting if the opponent has sweepers or heavy removal.
Use discard to delay their game plan while you stabilize and establish board presence.
late_game: Leverage massive card draw from Agatha to find answers or finishers
Continue sacrificing creatures to Tree for incremental damage
Close out games with efficient threats and resource superiority
Tree of Perdition as a game-winning threat, often fueled by Cauldron-generated value or graveyard recursion.
Secondary finishers include efficient removal clearing the board and a resilient midrange threat that can close out the game.
Go all-in when you have established Cauldron value, the opponent has no sweepers, or you can safely cast Tree of Perdition with protection/disruption ready.
Also commit when you can out-value them in trades and have the resources to follow up.

key_cards.name: Tree of Perdition
key_cards.role: Primary win condition and sacrifice outlet
key_cards.notes: Sacrifice creatures to deal direct damage to opponents, thinning your deck and applying pressure while maintaining board presence.

key_cards.name: Agatha's Soul Cauldron
key_cards.role: Card advantage engine and secondary sacrifice outlet
key_cards.notes: Sacrifice creatures to draw cards, enabling you to outlast opponents and find key interaction or finishers.

key_cards.name: Thoughtseize
key_cards.role: Early game disruption and tempo tool
key_cards.notes: Remove key threats or combo pieces from the opponent's hand, protecting your game plan and buying time to establish your sacrifice engine.

sequencing_tips: Hold up Thoughtseize for instant-speed disruption when possible
Sacrifice creatures to Tree of Perdition before casting new threats to maximize damage output
Use Agatha's Soul Cauldron to draw into answers before committing to large spells
Prioritize keeping one creature on the board to enable sacrifice abilities while maintaining combat pressure
Common mistakes include overcommitting to the Cauldron engine without board presence, failing to use Thoughtseize proactively, and mismanaging the graveyard as a resource.
Tree of Perdition likely scales with sacrifices or graveyard interactions, so sequence your plays to maximize its impact.
Watch for format-specific hate cards like Rest in Peace or Leyline of the Void, and adjust your sacrifice strategy accordingly.
Practice reading opponent hands to optimize Thoughtseize targets and avoid dead draws.

matchups.advice: Prioritize early Thoughtseize to remove key threats, then stabilize with Tree of Perdition to trade efficiently and deal direct damage.
Use Agatha's Soul Cauldron to draw through their hand and find answers, while sacrificing creatures to Tree to chip away at their life total before they stabilize.
Disrupt their key pieces with Thoughtseize immediately, then use your sacrifice outlets to thin your deck and find interaction quickly.

Use Thoughtseize to strip counterspells early. Cast threats in response to their spells, use cheap pingers or sacrifices to bypass counters, and rely on uncounterable effects or layered spells if available.

Apply early pressure with Thoughtseize to disrupt mana dorks. Use removal on key ramp spells. If they stabilize, transition to a grindier plan with Cauldron value and targeted removal to out-resource them.

Win the discard war. Prioritize Thoughtseize on their key pieces. Use Cauldron value to out-resource them. Protect Tree of Perdition with targeted removal and discard, and be willing to trade up in the late game.

common_threats: Card draw engines that outpace Agatha
Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Damnation), counterspells, and graveyard recursion.
Note if the opponent is playing token generators or ramp spells, as these dictate whether you need to race or grind.
Track their hand size and discard patterns to anticipate their next move.
